body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

meta.title: Network Time Security for the Unicast Mode of the Precision Time Protocol

synchronization depends on how accurate are the times times of the messages that the protocol is using absolute accuracy is not as important as symmetry in the errors in the receive and transmit timestamps if the errors are symmetric they cancel out in the calculation of the offset but the error needs to be stable to enable a stable synchronization of the clocks on a computer running a software implementation of the protocol there are few different places where the timestamps can be captured application timestamps are least accurate kernel and driver timestamps are more accurate and most accurate are hardware timestamps that's when the networking controller has its own clock for timestamping usually it's implemented in the mac at the physical layer it's much more accurate it's close to the actual wire or fiber but this is very uncommon especially at higher networking speeds next slide please here is an example of errors in kernel and driver timestamps as we can see they are not really randomly distributed and they are all kinds of bending that depends on networking load cpu load and all kinds of things next slide now this is an example of make hardware timestamps the absolute errors are now better than one microsecond and they are very stable there is an asymmetry but this can be compensated in software if necessary next slide please the problem for ntp is that some widely used networking cards cannot timestamp or receive packets it's a limitation in the hardware design usually the timestamping is limited to ptp messages sometimes the filter can be configured to a specific ptp version transport or message type support for ntp is very rare i think i saw only one chip that actually had a filter specific to ntp s4 the transmission usually any packet can be timestamp it's not an issue next slide please okay so the solution for ntp is obvious we can wrap ntp messages in ptp messages this way the hardware will generate a timestamp and the ntp implementation can use it for controlling the clock [Music] this is the proposal uses a valid ptp message it will not break normal ptp clocks if they receive this message for example due to a misconfiguration there is a possibility to use the ptp correction field but unica support in switches seems to be very rare and currently i don't think it's worth the trouble next slide please here is
